sections.heading: What is a Verb?
sections.body:
A verb is an action word or state of being.

Action: run, eat, write, think, study
State: is, am, are, was, were, have, seem

Every sentence needs a verb:
• "She runs." (action)
• "He is happy." (state)
• "They study English." (action)

sections.heading: Simple Present Tense
sections.body:
Used for: habits, facts, routines, general truths

I/You/We/They: base form
• I work. They play. We study.

He/She/It: add -s or -es
• He works. She plays. It rains.

Negatives: don't / doesn't + base form
• I don't work. He doesn't work.

Questions: Do/Does + subject + base form
• Do you work? Does he work?

sections.heading: Present Continuous Tense
sections.body:
Used for: actions happening right now, temporary situations, future plans

Formula: am/is/are + verb-ing
• I am working. She is studying.
• They are playing football.

Spelling rules for -ing:
• work → working (just add -ing)
• run → running (double last consonant)
• make → making (drop the e)
• study → studying (change y to i)

jargon.definition: An action word or state of being. Every sentence needs at least one verb. Examples: run, eat, is, seem, think.
The time form of a verb. English has 3 main tenses: past, present, future. Each has simple, continuous, and perfect forms.
The person or thing doing the action. In "She runs", "she" is the subject.
Changing a verb form to match tense, person, or number. "go" → "goes" (3rd person present).

summary: Start with Simple Present for habits and Present Continuous for now. Master the -s/-es rule for he/she/it — it's the most common beginner mistake.
